metadata.subject: Social Studies
metadata.description: Physical Geography 11 develops students' understanding of the Earth's physical systems — landforms, climate, weather, water, soils, and ecosystems — and the processes that shape them. Students examine the interactions among Earth's four spheres (lithosphere, hydrosphere, atmosphere, and biosphere) and analyze how these systems support life and shape human activity. The course emphasizes BC's distinctive physical geography and connects Earth systems science to contemporary issues of climate change, environmental stewardship, and natural hazards. First Peoples relationships with and knowledge of local landscapes are integrated throughout.
metadata.general_description: A course exploring Earth's physical systems — geomorphology, climate, water, soils, and ecosystems — with emphasis on BC landscapes, climate change, and First Peoples relationships with land.

big_ideas.statement: Earth's physical systems are dynamic and interconnected, shaping environments and supporting life.
big_ideas.elaboration: Students examine how the lithosphere, hydrosphere, atmosphere, and biosphere interact as complex, interdependent systems — and how changes in one system cascade through others.

big_ideas.statement: Landforms and landscapes are shaped by ongoing physical processes over vast spans of time.
big_ideas.elaboration: Students explore how tectonic forces, weathering, erosion, and deposition continuously reshape the Earth's surface — connecting these processes to the dramatic landscapes of BC.

big_ideas.statement: Climate and weather are shaped by complex interactions among Earth's systems and are changing due to human activity.
big_ideas.elaboration: Students develop a scientific understanding of climate and weather systems, examine the evidence for human-caused climate change, and analyze its implications for physical environments and human societies.

big_ideas.statement: Indigenous peoples have developed deep, place-based knowledge of physical environments through thousands of years of relationship.
big_ideas.elaboration: Students engage with Traditional Ecological Knowledge as a sophisticated system of environmental understanding — complementary to and distinct from Western scientific approaches.

competencies.raw: Apply geographic thinking, including spatial analysis, systems thinking, and scale, to understand physical processes; analyze cause and consequence in Earth systems; evaluate evidence for climate change and its impacts.
i_can_statements: I can apply geographic thinking — spatial analysis, systems thinking, and scale — to understand and explain physical processes.
I can analyze the causes and consequences of Earth system changes, including climate change.
I can evaluate scientific evidence for environmental change and connect it to physical and human consequences.
I can integrate Indigenous ecological knowledge with scientific approaches to understand physical environments.

competencies.raw: Communicate geographic knowledge using maps, diagrams, data visualizations, and written analysis; use precise geographic vocabulary; present findings about physical systems clearly and accurately.
i_can_statements: I can create and interpret maps, cross-sections, climate graphs, and data visualizations to communicate geographic information.
I can use precise physical geography vocabulary accurately in written and oral communication.
I can present geographic analysis of physical systems with evidence, clarity, and appropriate visual support.

competencies.raw: Recognize human impacts on physical systems and take responsibility for environmental stewardship; engage with Indigenous perspectives on land stewardship; consider the ethical dimensions of environmental decision-making.
i_can_statements: I can analyze human impacts on physical systems and evaluate strategies for environmental stewardship.
I can engage respectfully with Indigenous land stewardship practices and perspectives.
I can consider the ethical dimensions of environmental decisions — including climate justice and intergenerational responsibility.

areas.name: Earth Systems and Geomorphology
areas.raw: Earth's structure, plate tectonics, and landform-shaping processes.
topics: Earth's structure: crust, mantle, outer and inner core — composition and properties
Plate tectonics: theory, evidence, and types of plate boundaries
Volcanism and seismicity: volcanic types, eruptions, earthquake mechanics, and BC risk
Weathering and erosion: mechanical and chemical weathering, mass wasting, and erosional agents
Glaciation: glacial processes, landforms, and BC's glacial legacy
BC landforms: mountains, plateaus, fjords, and river systems — formation and significance

areas.name: Weather and Climate
areas.raw: Atmospheric systems, weather patterns, climate zones, and climate change.
topics: Atmospheric composition and structure: troposphere, stratosphere, and atmospheric layers
Energy budget: solar radiation, albedo, greenhouse effect, and heat transfer
Weather systems: pressure, wind, fronts, precipitation, and storm development
Climate classification: Koppen system, climate zones, and BC's diverse climates
Climate change: evidence, mechanisms (CO2, methane, feedback loops), and projections
Climate impacts: sea level rise, extreme weather, glacier retreat, and ecosystem shifts

areas.name: Water Systems
areas.raw: The hydrological cycle, river systems, groundwater, and oceans.
topics: Hydrological cycle: evaporation, condensation, precipitation, runoff, and infiltration
River systems: drainage basins, stream processes, floodplains, deltas, and river management
Groundwater: aquifers, wells, contamination risks, and sustainable management
Lakes and wetlands: formation, ecology, and the role of wetlands in BC's water systems
Ocean systems: ocean circulation, thermohaline conveyor, and ocean-climate interactions
Water security: global freshwater distribution, scarcity, and BC water management

areas.name: Soils and Ecosystems
areas.raw: Soil formation, biomes, ecosystems, and biodiversity.
topics: Soil formation: weathering, organic matter, soil horizons, and soil classification
Soil and agriculture: fertility, degradation, erosion, and sustainable soil management
Biomes: terrestrial biome classification and the climate-vegetation relationship
BC's diverse ecosystems: temperate rainforest, boreal forest, grassland, alpine, and coastal ecosystems
Biodiversity: species diversity, ecosystem services, and biodiversity threats
Ecological relationships: food webs, nutrient cycling, and ecosystem dynamics

areas.name: First Peoples and Environmental Stewardship
areas.raw: Indigenous relationships with land, TEK, and environmental stewardship.
topics: Traditional Ecological Knowledge: Indigenous understanding of local ecosystems, seasonal cycles, and species
Land-based practices: sustainable resource management, controlled burning, and fish management
Place names and geography: how First Peoples' place names encode ecological and historical knowledge
Environmental stewardship principles: Indigenous values of reciprocity, respect, and responsibility
Collaboration in conservation: Indigenous-led protected areas and co-management agreements in BC
Climate change impacts on Indigenous communities: land, food, and cultural implications
